identity.title: Motorcycle, ATV, and All Other Motor Vehicle Dealers

lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market full-service powersports dealers selling motorcycles, scooters, ATVs, side-by-sides, personal watercraft, snowmobiles, powered golf carts, and closely related vehicles while also supplying repeat repair, maintenance, parts, accessories, warranty, financing, insurance, or trade-in services to external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Pure merchandise retailers without a material repeat service relationship, aircraft-only dealers, utility-trailer-only dealers, online parts-only sellers, manufacturers, private-party sellers, captive internal units, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Primarily consumer vehicle sales with repeat ownership-lifecycle revenue from parts, accessories, service labor, repair, warranty administration, financing and insurance commissions, apparel, and future trade-ins. Unit sales are transactional and seasonal; service and ownership support form the recurring outsourced-service component.
lens.deviation_from_default: The NAICS code spans materially different vehicle categories, including motorcycles, ATVs, personal watercraft, utility trailers, aircraft, snowmobiles, and powered golf carts. It is narrowed to full-service powersports-oriented dealers so the operating workflow and repeat customer-service model are coherent.

a.bridge: Powersports-dealer sources identify specific automatable sales and CRM workflows but do not measure a wage-weighted task share. I map automated lead response, qualification, appointment booking, follow-up, conversation summaries, inventory answers, marketing, and routine paperwork to sales-support and administrative labor while assigning low exposure to technicians, parts handling, vehicle setup, appraisal, delivery, and accountable closing. Blended roles at small dealers can make the mapping overstate separable hours.
a.rationale: Purpose-built powersports CRM software already automates email and SMS response, inventory-specific questions, qualification, follow-up, summaries, and appointment booking. A public dealer group also reports meaningful compensation, advertising, and administrative costs, implying a material workflow base, but physical service and high-trust selling remain labor-intensive.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit occupation-and-wage table plus two-week task diaries from salespeople, BDC staff, service advisers, parts staff, F&I managers, title clerks, technicians, and general managers at 30-50 independent powersports dealers would materially move a.
caveats: No source directly measures wage-weighted AI exposure for NAICS 441227.
RumbleOn is a large multi-location operator rather than a representative LMM dealership.
The software source is a vendor capability description, not a realized labor study.

rho.rationale: More than half of exposed opportunity appears implementable within five years because powersports-specific CRM automation is commercially available and designed around existing dealer inventory and lead systems. Fragmented data, OEM systems, state licensing and finance compliance, staff turnover, seasonal peaks, and the need for human takeover constrain deployment and sustained labor avoidance.
rho.change_evidence: Matched dealer cohorts from CRM and DMS vendors showing adoption, payroll hours avoided, lead-response time, appointment conversion, compliance exceptions, error rates, and customer satisfaction over at least 24 months would materially narrow rho.
caveats: Vendor-reported response and conversion performance is not independently audited in the cited page.
Implementation may shift staff toward higher-value selling rather than reduce hiring.
The broad code includes dealer types with different software stacks and seasonal patterns.

e.rationale: Many franchised powersports dealers operate across the vehicle life cycle and sell parts, service, accessories, and F&I, but the NAICS definition also includes aircraft, utility-trailer, golf-cart, and other specialty sellers and permits retail without repair. The eligible full-service powersports share is therefore likely material but well below certain.
e.change_evidence: A business-register census of NAICS 441227 establishments and firms tagged by primary vehicle type, service bays, technician headcount, repair revenue, parts revenue, and F&I activity would directly measure eligibility.
caveats: The frozen n input is missing and is not replaced or estimated here.
Establishment activity does not map cleanly to firm-level eligibility.
The public dealer-group service mix likely overstates full-service capability among small specialty sellers.

s5.rationale: The powersports market is described as fragmented, and specialist succession advisers report more sellers entering the market and more dealerships available than during the pandemic. These observations support an active transfer market but provide neither an eligible-firm denominator nor closed-deal rate; a moderate five-year probability is bounded judgment.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of powersports dealership closings from specialist brokers and OEM franchise-transfer approvals, matched to the opening population and classified as third-party control sales, internal transitions, closures, or minority investments, would directly estimate s5.
caveats: Seller availability and broker commentary do not measure completed qualifying transfers.
The source does not quantify owner age, sale failures, or the population at risk.
Manufacturer consent and franchise rights can limit transferability.

q.rationale: Administrative and lead-management savings can be retained, especially in fixed-price service and fee-based F&I workflows, but vehicle pricing is transparent and competitive and OEM promotions influence customer economics. RumbleOn's 2024 data show parts, service, and accessories generated $94.5 million gross profit on $206.2 million revenue, while new-vehicle gross profit per vehicle fell 18%, illustrating stronger capture potential outside negotiated unit sales.
q.change_evidence: Matched location-level pricing and gross-margin panels for adopters and non-adopters, separated into new units, used units, service labor, parts, accessories, and F&I across several seasonal cycles, would materially change q.
caveats: RumbleOn's scale, brand mix, financing, and cost structure differ from independent LMM firms.
Observed category margins are not a direct measure of retention of AI-generated benefits.
Promotional support and inventory aging can quickly override operating-cost savings.

d5.bridge: Recent new-unit sales and a large dealer group's revenue proxy constant-price demand for the broader full-service powersports basket. I moderate the unit-sales decline because installed vehicles continue to need parts and service and because the NAICS lens spans off-highway categories beyond motorcycles. The proxy may overstate weakness if service offsets unit sales, or understate it if affordability and rider demographics cause a structural decline.
d5.rationale: US new motorcycle and scooter sales fell 7.6% in 2025 and ATV sales fell 3.5%; RumbleOn's 2024 powersports revenue fell 12.1% and parts, service, and accessories revenue fell 14.7%. These are meaningful demand headwinds, while the installed base, product-category diversity, and repair needs make a total collapse of the current service basket unlikely over five years.
d5.change_evidence: MIC category forecasts, registered-vehicle and rider-participation cohorts, average fleet age, real service work orders, technician hours, parts volume, and repeat-customer retention through 2031 would materially move d5.
caveats: Nominal revenue does not hold price and quality constant.
The MIC figures cover motorcycles, scooters, and ATVs but not every vehicle category in NAICS 441227.
Weather, tariffs, credit availability, interest rates, and OEM inventory cycles create large swings.

o.rationale: Most year-five service quantity should still need an accountable dealer because vehicles require physical inventory, setup, diagnosis, repair, parts fitment, warranty processing, trade appraisal, delivery, licensing, and finance compliance. AI can remove initial lead response and routine follow-up, and online acquisition can bypass some local sourcing, but these tools still hand customers and exceptions to dealership staff.
o.change_evidence: OEM and marketplace data showing direct-to-consumer transaction share, mobile-service penetration, remotely completed financing and titling, and the percentage of service work completed without a dealership operator would materially move o.
caveats: Direct OEM sales, mobile mechanics, and online used-vehicle platforms could expand faster than assumed.
Operator need is much lower for marketing and search than for repair, delivery, and regulated transaction steps.
Rules and OEM franchise arrangements vary by state and vehicle category.

narrative.executive_view: The coherent opportunity is a full-service powersports dealer, not the entire catch-all NAICS category. AI can automate a meaningful layer of lead handling and sales administration, while physical vehicles, repairs, warranty work, and regulated closing keep the operator central. The industry currently faces material unit-demand and affordability pressure.
narrative.ai_impact: Powersports-specific CRM tools already answer and qualify leads, reference live inventory and customer history, manage email and SMS follow-up, summarize conversations, and book appointments. These capabilities target BDC, sales-support, and administrative hours, but not technicians, parts handling, setup, appraisal, delivery, or accountable F&I approval.
narrative.value_capture: Savings should be more retainable in service, parts, and fee workflows than in negotiated vehicle sales. Transparent listings, customer comparison shopping, OEM promotions, inventory carrying costs, and seasonal discounting are strong pass-through channels.
narrative.firm_availability: Fragmentation and rising seller interest support potential control transfers, but the frozen LMM firm count is missing and no source supplies a denominator-based transfer rate. The broad code also makes both eligibility and addressable-firm availability particularly uncertain.
narrative.demand_durability: New motorcycle, scooter, and ATV sales weakened in 2025, following a substantial decline at a major dealer group in 2024. Installed vehicles still require service and parts, and off-highway categories diversify the basket, but five-year demand should be underwritten conservatively.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Major risks are prolonged affordability pressure, aging or shrinking rider cohorts, tariffs, OEM distress, franchise-transfer restrictions, seasonal working capital, weather, poor dealer data, and the code's mix of unrelated specialty vehicles. The most important missing evidence is a dealer census by activity and a closed-transfer denominator.
narrative.principal_driver: Commercially available, powersports-specific AI can absorb repetitive lead response, qualification, follow-up, scheduling, and sales-support work.
narrative.principal_weakness: Demand is declining and highly seasonal, while the frozen firm count is missing and the catch-all industry definition prevents a clean eligibility denominator.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 441227 Motorcycle, ATV, and All Other Motor Vehicle Dealers
sources.supports: Defines the industry to include motorcycles, scooters, ATVs, personal watercraft, utility trailers, aircraft, snowmobiles, powered golf carts, and other vehicles, with or without repair and parts activities; supports the heterogeneous lens and eligibility caveat.

sources.title: DP360 AI for Specialty Dealerships
sources.supports: Documents powersports-specific automated email and SMS lead response, inventory-aware answers, qualification, follow-up, summaries, appointment handling, and human takeover; also reports vendor performance data across thousands of leads.

sources.title: RumbleOn, Inc. 2024 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Describes a highly fragmented powersports market and dealership life-cycle services; reports 2024 revenue, gross profit, vehicle counts, compensation and other expenses, seasonality, ancillary-service economics, and regulatory constraints.

sources.title: Shifting Gears: Planning for Powersports Exit & Succession in Today's Environment, Part 1
sources.supports: Reports specialist broker observations that more sellers were entering the powersports market, buyers had slowed, and more dealerships were available than during the pandemic, while emphasizing valuation and financing constraints.

sources.title: MIC reports 2025 motorcycle, ATV retail sales decline
sources.supports: Reports Motorcycle Industry Council data showing 2025 US new motorcycle and scooter sales down 7.6% and ATV sales down 3.5%, with motorcycles and scooters representing 73.3% of tracked new-unit sales.
